paper_title: Bermuda Platform Sedimentology and Stratigraphy Methods

methods.description: Recording shallow seismic data using a boomer system from a boat to image subsurface sediment layers and structures. This method involved setting up equipment, running traverses, recording data, and subsequent processing and interpretation.

steps.description: Shallow seismics were recorded in August and September 1979 on a 11.4 m long boat (RV Micmac) using a boomer system. It consisted of a Uniboom model 230-1, an energy transformer EG&G model 234, a hydrophone EG&G model 265 and an EPC model 3200 graphic recorder.
steps.searchable_summary: Set up and deploy shallow seismic boomer system equipment on research vessel

steps.description: Fifteen traverses radiating away from the Bermuda islands, two transverse sections across North Lagoon, and two traverses parallel to the islands and the northern reef margin were recorded, respectively ( Figure 3). The total length of seismic sections amounts to ca 200 km.
steps.searchable_summary: Navigate research vessel along predefined seismic traverse lines

steps.description: The reflection seismic data were not stored digitally. Best results were obtained with boat speeds ranging from 3 to 5 knots. In North Lagoon, 4 impulses per second and 16 sweeps were commonly used, which produced a vertical resolution to 0.2 m.
steps.searchable_summary: Record seismic reflection data using graphic recorder with specified parameters (boat speed, impulses, sweeps)

steps.description: To determine the depth of the sediment surface, a scale for the printouts was made for the most often used frequency (16 sweeps). Sonic speeds in water, sediments, and rocks were estimated. A sonic velocity of 1,540 m/sec was assumed for water at 36.5‰ and 26°C (Smith, 1974). For the unconsolidated Holocene sediments, a sonic velocity 10% higher than that in water was assumed. In hardened Pleistocene sands and clays, sonic velocities as high as 2,000 m/sec were used, while the sonic velocity in peat is rather low (ca 1,000 m/sec).
steps.searchable_summary: Estimate sonic velocities for different materials (water, sediment, rock) and create depth scale for seismic printouts

steps.description: Unwanted signals had to be identified and excluded, and comprise multiple reflections, reverberations ("singing"), side echos and point reflections.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and exclude unwanted signals (multiples, reverberations, side echos, point reflections) from seismic profiles

steps.description: The analysis of seismic sequences included the identification of discontinuity surfaces, i.e., surfaces characterized by strong reflectors as well as potential internal structures, determination of outer shapes and the spatial relationships of units.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze seismic profiles to identify discontinuity surfaces, internal structures, unit shapes, and spatial relationships

steps.description: The identified sequences are supposed to match a depositional sequence in core.
steps.searchable_summary: Correlate identified seismic sequences with depositional sequences observed in sediment cores

steps.description: Interpretations of seismic sections were corrected for boat speed, sonic speed in various materials, and by taking into account water depth soundings and thickness measurements in cores.
steps.searchable_summary: Correct seismic interpretations based on boat speed, sonic velocity assumptions, water depth soundings, and core thickness measurements

methods.description: Collection of subsurface sediment cores using a vibrocorer and surface sediment samples using a grab sampler and from beaches, along with recording sample locations and water depths.

steps.description: Thirty-nine vibrocores were taken with a high-energy pneumatic vibrocorer (Meischner, Torunski, & Kuhn, 1981) utilizing 6 m long, 8.5 cm diameter aluminium from a floating raft ( Figure 3; Table 1) during September and October 1980.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect vibrocores using a pneumatic vibrocorer from a floating raft

steps.description: There was an average compaction of 25% during coring, which was corrected for in core logs.
steps.searchable_summary: Record and correct for sediment compaction during vibrocoring

steps.description: Also, in the fall of 1980, 18 surface sediment samples from North Lagoon were collected with a Van Veen-grab sampler ( Figure 3; Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect surface sediment samples using a Van Veen-grab sampler

steps.description: In addition, eight surface sediment samples were taken from three beaches along the north shore of the Bermuda islands ( Figure 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect surface sediment samples from beaches

steps.description: Positioning during sampling was performed by measuring angles between landmarks and nautical signs with a sextant, and by sounding with a compass and an angle prisma overlaid on nautical charts of the Bermuda platform 1 : 6,200 to 1 : 60,000 were used.
steps.searchable_summary: Determine sample station positions using sextant, compass, and nautical charts

steps.description: At surface sediment sample stations, water depths were measured using an Elac echoscope LAZ 36; depths were checked with the scale on the grab sampler rope.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure water depth at surface sample stations using echoscope and grab sampler rope scale

steps.description: At the core stations, water depths were measured with a chain leadline and an echo sounder (Fahrentholz model V30/150-S-T).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure water depth at core stations using chain leadline and echo sounder

methods.description: Initial laboratory processing of collected vibrocore samples, including opening, cutting, photographing, and describing the cores.

steps.description: At the home laboratory, aluminium core pipes were opened lengthwise with a saw and the core cut with a knife and subsequently photographed, described, and analysed.
steps.searchable_summary: Open core pipes lengthwise using a saw

steps.description: At the home laboratory, aluminium core pipes were opened lengthwise with a saw and the core cut with a knife and subsequently photographed, described, and analysed.
steps.searchable_summary: Cut core lengthwise using a knife

steps.description: At the home laboratory, aluminium core pipes were opened lengthwise with a saw and the core cut with a knife and subsequently photographed, described, and analysed.
steps.searchable_summary: Photograph core sections

steps.description: At the home laboratory, aluminium core pipes were opened lengthwise with a saw and the core cut with a knife and subsequently photographed, described, and analysed.
steps.searchable_summary: Describe core lithology, structures, and other features

steps.description: At the home laboratory, aluminium core pipes were opened lengthwise with a saw and the core cut with a knife and subsequently photographed, described, and analysed.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare core samples for subsequent analyses (e.g., age dating, texture)

methods.description: Determination of absolute ages for carbonate, peat, and coral samples from cores using radiocarbon and Uranium-thorium dating methods, including necessary calibrations and corrections.

steps.description: Absolute age data from carbonates and from peat (n = 23) were obtained promptly after core opening by radiocarbon dating (Dr. H. Erlenkeuser, University of Kiel, Germany).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform radiocarbon dating on selected carbonate and peat samples (external lab)

steps.description: These ages were recently calibrated using Beta-Cal 3.18 provided by Beta Analytic Inc. (Miami) and relying on the INTCAL 13 and MARINE13 databases (Ramsey, 2009;Reimer et al., 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Calibrate radiocarbon ages using Beta-Cal software and INTCAL/MARINE databases

steps.description: A Delta R-value of −129 ± 29 has been used for Bermuda (Druffel, 1997) in order to correct for correction of the reservoir effect.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Delta R correction for marine reservoir effect to calibrated radiocarbon ages

steps.description: Prior to age dating, X-ray diffraction was used to identify aragonitic coral samples.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform X-ray diffraction (XRD) on coral samples to identify aragonite

steps.description: Uranium-thorium-ages of Pleistocene corals (n = 6) were provided promptly after core opening by Dr. P. Schwarcz (McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada) and by Dr. R. Harmon (Scottish University Research and Reactor Centre, Glasgow, UK) using the alpha-counting method.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Uranium-thorium dating on aragonitic coral samples using alpha-counting method (external lab)

methods.description: Analysis of grain size distribution for sediment samples from cores, surface samples, and beaches using sieving and sedimentation techniques, followed by calculation of statistical parameters and assignment of textural classes.

steps.description: The textures of a total of 160 sediment samples from cores, 18 surface samples from North Lagoon, and 8 beach sediments were analysed by sieving and sedimentation techniques. Core samples for analyses were selected based on visible facies variation.
steps.searchable_summary: Select sediment samples from cores, surface samples, and beaches for texture analysis

steps.description: The textures of a total of 160 sediment samples from cores, 18 surface samples from North Lagoon, and 8 beach sediments were analysed by sieving and sedimentation techniques.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze sediment texture using sieving and sedimentation techniques

steps.description: The samples were separated into 22 grain-size fractions. Here, the common fractions >2, 2-1, 1-0.5, 0.5-0.25, 0.25-0.125, 0.125-0.063, and <0.063 mm are used and documented.
steps.searchable_summary: Separate samples into specified grain size fractions and document results

steps.description: Mean grain size and sorting were recently calculated using the software Gradistat (Blott & Pye, 2001) and the formulas of Folk and Ward (1957).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate mean grain size using Gradistat software and Folk and Ward formulas

steps.description: Mean grain size and sorting were recently calculated using the software Gradistat (Blott & Pye, 2001) and the formulas of Folk and Ward (1957).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate sorting (standard deviation) using Gradistat software and Folk and Ward formulas

steps.description: Based on the amount of fines (<125 μm), Dunham (1962)-equivalent textures were assigned recently using 50% fines as the cut-off point between grain-supported and mud-supported textures. Grainstones have <10%, packstone 10%-50%, wackestone 50%-90% and mudstone >90% fines in this system.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate percentage of fines (<125 μm) and assign Dunham-equivalent textural classes

methods.description: Quantification of the types and relative abundance of skeletal and non-skeletal grains in selected sediment samples using binocular microscopy, including identification of mollusc species.

steps.description: Grain composition was quantified in 12 core samples, representing different facies, by identifying and counting (usually several hundred) grains of the 2-0.063 mm fractions under a binocular microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: Select core samples representing different facies for grain composition analysis

steps.description: Grain composition was quantified in 12 core samples, representing different facies, by identifying and counting (usually several hundred) grains of the 2-0.063 mm fractions under a binocular microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare 2-0.063 mm grain size fractions from selected samples

steps.description: Grain composition was quantified in 12 core samples, representing different facies, by identifying and counting (usually several hundred) grains of the 2-0.063 mm fractions under a binocular microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and count grain types (skeletal, non-skeletal) in 2-0.063 mm fractions using a binocular microscope

steps.description: The grain-size fractions >2 mm of three samples were used to identify mollusc species based on standard references such as Waller (1973) and Abbott (1974).
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare >2 mm grain size fractions from selected samples

steps.description: The grain-size fractions >2 mm of three samples were used to identify mollusc species based on standard references such as Waller (1973) and Abbott (1974).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify mollusc species from >2 mm fractions using standard references

methods.description: Application of statistical tests to analyze the collected and processed data.

steps.description: Statistical tests were made using the software Past (Hammer, Harper, & Ryan, 2001).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform statistical tests on data using Past software
inputs: Processed data (e.g., age data, texture data, composition data)
